creator: trevor@jpj.net
text:
Thank you for responding to my PR.  Looking through the commit logs, I see
that my xkbmap patch has been committed by Kris Kennaway, but Thomas
Dickey's xterm fix (patch-j in my PR) is being delayed.  At least, I don't
see it in the XFree86-4 port, nor the new XFree86-4-clients port.

> There might be more security problems.
> The real problem is running it setuid root, and not with a wrapper, as
> the 3.x ports did, which is also the correct fix.

The earlier ports simply use the wrapper that was shipped with XFree86.  
Unfortunately, XFree86 4.0 doesn't include such a program, so we'd have to
add one.  I've started work on adding back the wrapper from 3.3.6--if
you'd like to help, please let me know.  However, a wrapper is not a
panacea.  For instance, the original report of this bug said:

  XFree86 3.3.6 (and probably 4.0.0 as well ;) - by running X server (no
  matter it's setuid, or called from setuid Xwrapper - works in both cases,
  seems to me Xwrapper in default RH 6.x distro is rather dumb ;) with
  -xkbmap parameter and over 2100 of 'A's (or shellcode, again, it's rather
  trivial to exploit :), you'll get beautiful overflow with root privledges
  in main (Xserver) process...

There are other approaches that would improve the security of this port.  
For instance, the X server could be installed without the setuid bit and
run from xdm.  Users who depend on a startx script could be warned about
the possibility of undiscovered security bugs, reminded of the existence
of XFree86 3.3.6, and told to set the bit themselves if they need 4.0 and
accept the risk.  More ambitiously, the X server could use video drivers
in the kernel.  After looking at the sys/dev/fb sources I concluded this
won't be happening soon.

> That of course doesn't mean, that your patch is incorrrect, I only
> say, that it still should kept FORBIDDEN.

I poked around a bit but didn't find a definite policy about forbidding
stuff for security reasons.  Looking at the other ports that were in that
state a few days ago, I see they all had specific bugs:

===>  hylafax-4.0.2 is forbidden: Security hole (buffer overflow
yielding setuid uucp).
===>  ja-lynx-2.8.2.rel1 is forbidden: Riddled with buffer overflows
exploitable by a malicious server to execute code as the local
user..
===>  ja-lynx-2.8.3.dev16 is forbidden: Riddled with buffer overflows
exploitable by a malicious server to execute code as the local
user..
===>  mh-6.8.4 is forbidden: Buffer overflow in MIME parsing code,
exploitable via email. Since mh is no longer actively developed,
this bug may not get fixed - consider using the nmh port in
/usr/ports/mail/nmh, which is the successor to mh.
===>  bb-1.2a is forbidden: Remote buffer overflow in bbd.
===>  lynx-ssl-2.8.2.1 is forbidden: Riddled with buffer overflows
exploitable by a malicious server to execute code as the local
user.
===>  kdebase-1.90 is forbidden: Local buffer overflow yielding
gid nogroup.

I also see:

  @Nuke beroftpd, it has security holes and active development seems to
  have stopped. Anyone interested in fixing this can revive it.
 
  --Bill Fumerola in ports/ftp/beroftpd/Attic/Makefile,v

  @Remove the FORBIDDEN tag on lynx-current. Everyone is encouraged to
  upgrade to this version, which fixes all of the known problems with
  previous lynx versions.

  --Andrey A. Chernov in ports/www/lynx-current/Makefile,v

It looks to me as though it's usual to forbid ports when there are
known--not just theoretical--security bugs, and to allow them again if
those bugs get fixed.  I can't fault you for being cautious, but FreeBSD
custom seems more relaxed.

> Did you back-contribute your patches to the XFree86 authors?

Yes.  This is all I've gotten back:

Date: Fri, 2 Jun 2000 08:59:34 +0000
From: owner-fixes@x.physics.usyd.edu.au
Reply-To: patchers@xfree86.org
To: Trevor Johnson <trevor@jpj.net>
Subject: Re: (fixes seq: A.91) patch for -xkbmap overflow in XFree86 4.0

  --- This is an automatically generated reply ---

The patch you have submitted to fixes@XFree86.org has been
assigned the sequence number A.91.

Thanks for your submission.
--
Trevor Johnson
http://jpj.net/~trevor/gpgkey.txt
